exercises.title: Cross-attention: text queries, vision answers
exercises.prompt: Cross-attention lets every text token attend to every patch token: the text stream provides queries, the vision stream provides keys and values. Build cross-attention where text queries attend over vision keys/values, and show a text token grounding in the most relevant patch. Discuss how this differs from self-attention.
exercises.hint: Q from text tokens, K and V from vision patches. attention(Q_text, K_vision, V_vision) -> each text token attends to patches. Show a text token attending most to its relevant patch. vs self-attention: self uses one stream for Q/K/V; cross uses text for Q, vision for K/V.
exercises.solution:
Cross-attention grounds text in vision: the text queries, the vision keys and values answer.

```python
import math
def softmax(xs):
    m = max(xs); e = [math.exp(x-m) for x in xs]; s = sum(e); return [x/s for x in e]

def cross_attention(text_query, vision_keys, vision_values, d):
    scores = [sum(q*k for q, k in zip(text_query, key))/math.sqrt(d) for key in vision_keys]
    weights = softmax(scores)
    out = [sum(w*v[j] for w, v in zip(weights, vision_values)) for j in range(len(vision_values[0]))]
    return weights, out

text_query = [1, 0]                 # the word 'cat' queries
vision_keys = [[1, 0], [0, 1]]      # patch 0 (cat region), patch 1 (background)
vision_values = [[9, 0], [0, 9]]
weights, out = cross_attention(text_query, vision_keys, vision_values, d=2)
print('attention over patches:', [round(w, 2) for w in weights])
print('grounded output:', [round(o, 1) for o in out])
```

Output (approximately):

```
attention over patches: [0.66, 0.34]
grounded output: [5.9, 3.1]
```

**How cross-attention differs from self-attention.** Self-attention (Lesson 33) uses *one* stream for queries, keys, and values — tokens attend to each other within the same sequence. *Cross*-attention uses *two* streams: the *text* provides queries ("what am I looking for?") and the *vision* provides keys and values ("here is what is in the image"). So each text token attends over the *patches*, grounding the word in the relevant image region — the word "cat" attends most to the cat-region patch (weight 0.66). "The text queries; the vision keys and values answer." This is how a word gets grounded in a region. The next exercises compose the decoder block and get the masks right.

exercises.title: A decoder block: causal self-attention + cross-attention + FFN
exercises.prompt: A VL decoder block composes causal text self-attention, cross-attention to vision, and a feed-forward, each with residuals. Build the block in order (self-attn -> cross-attn -> FFN) and show a text token being refined by its text context AND the image. Discuss why the order matters.
exercises.hint: Block: h1 = x + causal_self_attn(norm(x)); h2 = h1 + cross_attn(norm(h1), vision); out = h2 + ffn(norm(h2)). Self-attn first (text context), then cross-attn (ground in image), then FFN. Show a token refined by both. Order: integrate text context before grounding in vision.
exercises.solution:
A VL decoder block composes causal self-attention, cross-attention, and FFN, each with a residual.

```python
def layernorm(x):
    m = sum(x)/len(x); v = sum((xi-m)**2 for xi in x)/len(x)
    return [(xi-m)/(v**0.5+1e-5) for xi in x]

def decoder_block(token, self_attn, cross_attn, ffn):
    h1 = [a+b for a, b in zip(token, self_attn(layernorm(token)))]    # 1. causal text self-attn
    h2 = [a+b for a, b in zip(h1, cross_attn(layernorm(h1)))]         # 2. cross-attn to vision
    return [a+b for a, b in zip(h2, ffn(layernorm(h2)))]             # 3. FFN

token = [1.0, 2.0]
self_attn = lambda x: [0.1, 0.1]      # text context contribution
cross_attn = lambda x: [0.2, 0.2]     # vision grounding contribution
ffn = lambda x: [0.05, 0.05]
print('token before:', token)
print('token after block:', [round(x, 2) for x in decoder_block(token, self_attn, cross_attn, ffn)])
```

Output:

```
token before: [1.0, 2.0]
token after block: [1.35, 2.35]
```

**Why this order.** The decoder block refines each text token in three steps: (1) *causal self-attention* integrates the *text context* (what other words came before), (2) *cross-attention* grounds the token in the *image* (which patches are relevant), and (3) the *FFN* processes the combined representation. The order matters: the token first gathers its text context, *then* grounds that contextualized representation in the image — so the vision grounding is informed by the surrounding words (the word "it" cross-attends to vision *after* resolving what "it" refers to from text context). Each step has a residual, so the token accumulates refinements. The final exercise gets the mask shapes right.

exercises.title: The mask shapes that keep both attentions legal
exercises.prompt: Two attentions need different masks: causal self-attention must mask future text tokens, but cross-attention must NOT mask any vision tokens (all patches are visible). Build both mask shapes and show the causal mask blocking future text while the cross-attention mask leaves all vision visible. Discuss why mixing them up breaks the model.
exercises.hint: Causal self-attn mask: text token i can attend to text tokens <= i (future masked). Cross-attn mask: text token i attends to ALL vision patches (none masked -- the whole image is available). Show both. Mixing up: a causal mask on cross-attn would hide most of the image; no mask on self-attn would let text see the future.
exercises.solution:
The two attentions need different masks — causal for text self-attention, no mask for vision cross-attention.

```python
def causal_self_mask(text_pos, n_text):
    # text token may attend to text tokens <= its position (future masked)
    return [1 if j <= text_pos else 0 for j in range(n_text)]

def cross_attn_mask(n_vision):
    # ALL vision patches are visible -- no masking
    return [1] * n_vision

print('causal self-attn (text token at pos 1, 3 text tokens):', causal_self_mask(1, 3))
print('cross-attn (4 vision patches):', cross_attn_mask(4))
print('-> self-attn masks the future; cross-attn leaves the whole image visible')
```

Output:

```
causal self-attn (text token at pos 1, 3 text tokens): [1, 1, 0]
cross-attn (4 vision patches): [1, 1, 1, 1]
-> self-attn masks the future; cross-attn leaves the whole image visible
```

**Why different masks.** *Causal self-attention* over text must mask *future* tokens (Lesson 33): the text is generated autoregressively, so a token at position 1 may attend to positions 0 and 1 but *not* position 2 (the future it has not generated yet) — `[1, 1, 0]`. But *cross-attention* to vision must mask *nothing*: the *entire image is available* when generating each word (you can see the whole picture while writing a caption), so all 4 patches are visible — `[1, 1, 1, 1]`. *Mixing them up breaks the model*: a causal mask on cross-attention would hide most of the image (the model could only see the first patches), and *no* mask on text self-attention would let the model *cheat by seeing future tokens* during training. The mask shapes encode the fundamental asymmetry: text is sequential (causal), vision is fully available (unmasked).

**The cross-attention synthesis.** Cross-attention grounds text in vision — text queries, vision keys/values answer (Ex. 1); the decoder block composes causal self-attention, then cross-attention, then FFN, so grounding is informed by text context (Ex. 2); and the two attentions use different masks — causal for text, unmasked for vision (Ex. 3). "Every text token attends to every patch token, so the model can ground each word in a region" — cross-attention is how the projection's coarse alignment (Lesson 60) becomes fine-grained word-to-region grounding. The next lesson trains the whole stack.
